title: Export Development Canada v. Unix Packaging, LLC
text:
CISG-online 7751 
Jurisdiction 
U.S.A. 
Tribunal 
U.S. District Court for the Central District of California 
Date of the decision 
12 December 2025 
Case no./docket no. 
CV 25-9097-DMG (JCx) 
Case name 
Export Development Canada v. Unix Packaging, LLC 
 
Order Re Plaintiff’s Motion for Default Judgment 
On September 24, 2025, Plaintiff Export Development Canada («EDC») filed a Complaint 
against Defendant Unix Packaging, LLC («Unix»), alleging four causes of action: (1) breach of 
contract; (2) action for the price; (3) account stated; and, (4) open book account. 
On September 30, 2025, EDC served Unix with the summons and Complaint. Upon EDC’s 
request, the Clerk of Court entered default against Unix on October 27, 2025. EDC filed a 
motion for default judgment against Unix on November 10, 2025. The Court held a hearing on 
this motion on December 12, 2025. EDC appeared, but Unix did not. For the following reasons, 
the Court GRANTS EDC’s MDJ in part. 
I. Factual Background 
EDC is a Canadian insurer and non-profit Canadian Crown corporation established by the 
Canadian Export Development Act of 1985. EDC’s headquarters are in Canada. EDC provides 
insurance against nonpayment to Canadian exporters who sell goods in the United States. 
Kefiplant Inc. («Kefiplant»), the non-party insured, is a kombucha supplier.1 Unix is a beverage 
wholesaler. Unix is an LLC whose sole member is a citizen of California. 
Unix ordered kombucha (the «Goods») from Kefiplant, which delivered the Goods to Unix 
between January and July of 2024. Kefiplant issued 30 invoices to Unix seeking payment for 
the Goods. The invoices total $835,418.60. As identified in the invoices, Unix ordered the 
Goods, the Goods were delivered to Unix, Unix accepted delivery of the Goods, and the 
invoices were received by Unix at or around the time they are dated. Unix did not reject the 
Goods, identify or notify Kefiplant of any defects or nonconformities in the Goods, return the 
Goods, or otherwise object to the Goods.2 
 
 
1 EDC brings this action as the insurer and assignee of Kefiplant. Kefiplant assigned its contractual rights against 
Unix to EDC. 
2 On an invoice dated February 7, 2024, it appears Kefiplant issued a $1,600.00 credit to Unix for «DAMAGED 
TOTE IN THE TRANSPORT.» This invoice is not necessarily contrary to EDC’s allegation that Unix did not object to 
or notify Kefiplant of any deformities of the goods, however, because it is possible Kefiplant independently 
detected the damaged Goods and issued a credit proactively. 
1 
 
2 
 
3 
 


CISG-online 7751 
 
2 
 
Unix did not pay the invoices except for a partial payment of $4,706.40 applied to Invoice 
No. 2592. Payment of the invoices was due between February and August 2024.  
EDC emailed a copy of the entry of default in this action to the principals of Unix and Unix 
employees on October 27, 2025. EDC received two «read» receipts indicating the Unix 
employees viewed the email. EDC also served a copy of the subject motion for default 
judgment and supporting documents on Unix. 
II. Discussion 
A. Federal Rule of Civil Procedure 55(b)(2) and Local Rule 55-1 Compliance 
EDC has complied with the procedural requirements applicable to default judgments. EDC has 
shown the Eitel factors weigh in favor of entering a default judgment against Unix. See Eitel v. 
McCool, 782 F.2d 1470, 1472 (9th Cir. 1986). In Eitel, the Ninth Circuit set forth a number of 
factors that courts may consider when evaluating a default judgment motion: (1) the 
possibility of prejudice to the plaintiff; (2) the merits of the plaintiff’s substantive claim; (3) the 
sufficiency of the complaint; (4) the sum of money at stake in the action; (5) the possibility of 
a dispute concerning material facts; (6) whether the default was due to excusable neglect; and 
(7) the strong policy underlying the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure favoring decisions on the 
merits. Id. at 1471–72. 
Since a default has been entered against Unix, the Court presumes that Plaintiff’s allegations 
are true. Fair Hous. Of Marin v. Combs, 285 F.3d 899, 906 (9th Cir. 2002); see also Geddes v. 
United Fin. Grp., 559 F.2d 557, 560 (9th Cir. 1977) («[U]pon default[,] the factual allegations 
of the complaint, except those relating to the amount of damages, will be taken as true.»). 
B. The Eitel Factors 
1. The Possibility of Prejudice to EDC 
Unix has not appeared or otherwise responded to the Complaint within the time permitted. 
Failure to enter a default judgment will prejudice EDC because it would deny EDC the right to 
judicial resolution of its claim «without other recourse for recovery.» Elektra Entm’t Grp., Inc. 
v. Crawford, 226 F.R.D. 388, 392 (C.D. Cal. 2005); see also PepsiCo, Inc. v. California Sec. Cans, 
238 F. Supp. 2d 1172, 1177 (C.D. Cal. 2002) («If Plaintiffs’ motion for default judgment is not 
granted, Plaintiffs will likely be without other recourse for recovery.»). The first Eitel factor 
therefore weighs in favor of default judgment. 
2. The Merits of EDC’s Substantive Claim and Sufficiency of the Complaint 
The second and third Eitel factors require EDC to state a claim on which it may recover. 
See PepsiCo, Inc., 238 F. Supp. 2d at 1175. In the Complaint, EDC alleges claims for: (1) breach 
of contract; (2) action for the price; (3) account stated; and, (4) open book account. 
The United Nations Convention on Contracts for the International Sale of Goods («CISG») 
applies to «contracts of sale of goods between parties whose places of business are in 
4 
 
5 
 
6 
 
7 
 
8 
 
9 
 
10  


CISG-online 7751 
 
3 
 
different States ... when the States are Contracting States.» Chateau des Charmes Wines Ltd. 
v. Sabate USA Inc., 328 F.3d 528, 530 (9th Cir. 2003) (quoting CISG, art. 1(1)(a), 15 U.S.C.App., 
52 Fed.Reg. 6262 (March 2, 1987)). The United States and Canada are contracting states to 
the CISG. Id. The CISG «governs only the formation of the contract of sale and the rights and 
obligations of the seller and buyer arising from such a contract.» CISG, art. 4. Parties may opt 
out of the CISG. CISG, art. 6. Parties are bound by agreed upon usage and past practices. CISG, 
art. 9. 
«A contract of sale need not be concluded in or evidenced by writing and is not subject to any 
other requirement as to form.» Chateau des Charmes Wines Ltd., 328 F.3d at 531 (quoting 
CISG, art. 11). «A proposal is an offer if it is sufficiently definite to indicate the goods and 
expressly or implicitly fix or make provision for determining the quantity and the price ... and 
it demonstrates an intention by the offeror to be bound if the proposal is accepted.» Id. 
(quotation marks and punctuation removed) (quoting CISG, art. 14). «[A]n offer is accepted if 
the offeree makes a statement or other conduct indicating assent to an offer.» Id. (quotation 
marks and punctuation removed) (quoting CISG, art. 18). «A contract is concluded at the 
moment when an acceptance of an offer becomes effective.» CISG, art. 23. A buyer «must pay 
the price for the goods and take delivery of them as required by the contract [and the CISG].» 
CISG, art. 53. 
Here, EDC has sufficiently alleged a contract between Kefiplant and Unix. Unix «ordered» the 
Goods from Kefiplant, Kefiplant delivered the Goods to Unix, and Unix accepted the Goods. 
The invoices are itemized and reflect set prices for specific quantities of the Goods. See, e.g. 
Doc. # 1-1 at 2 (charging $1,600,00 for 1.60 units of Kefiviva Living Organic Kombucha 12 G/L). 
Accord Chateau des Charmes Wines Ltd., 328 F.3d at 530-531 (finding a CISG contract where 
the parties made an oral agreement as to the quantity, kind, and price of corks, and the corks 
were delivered as reflected in invoices).3 Indeed, although EDC does not allege a written 
agreement between Kefiplant and Unix, the CISG does not require that the contract be 
memorialized in writing. Moreover, EDC has not alleged, and there is no evidence in the record 
to suggest, the parties opted out of the CISG, or that the parties had any past practices 
between them. Unix has breached the contract by failing to pay the price for the goods. 
Because EDC has successfully pleaded its breach of contract claim, the Court need not analyze 
the sufficiency of the pleadings as to the other causes of action. 
 
 
3 EDC cites to a case where the district court found the CISG does not set forth the elements of a breach of 
contract claim. Urica, Inc. v. Pharmaplast S.A.E., No. CV 11-02476-MMM (RZx), 2014 WL 3893372, at *10 
(C.D. Cal. Aug. 8, 2014), aff'd sub nom. Urica, Inc. v. Medline Indus., Inc., 669 F. App'x 421 (9th Cir. 2016). Instead, 
the district court applied the «universal elements» of a breach of contract claim: «formation, performance, 
breach and damages.» Id. Although the Court follows the Ninth Circuit’s approach in Chateau des Charmes Wines 
Ltd., as it is reported binding authority, accord Barbara Berry, S.A. de C.V. v. Ken M. Spooner Farms, Inc., 
254 F. App'x 646, 647 (9th Cir. 2007) (reversing and remanding for the district court to «apply[] the CISG» to 
determine whether a contract was formed), EDC has also alleged a contract under the «universal elements.» 
11  
12  
13  


CISG-online 7751 
 
4 
 
3. Remining Eitel Factors 
The remaining Eitel factors are: (4) the sum of money at stake in the action; (5) the possibility 
of a dispute concerning material facts; (6) whether the default was due to excusable neglect; 
and (7) the strong policy underlying the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure favoring decisions on 
the merits. Eitel, 782 F.2d at 1471–72. 
EDC claims a liquidated4 sum certain of $835,418.60 representative of the invoiced amount of 
the Goods.5 This sum claimed is reasonably proportionate to the harm caused by Unix’s 
conduct. PepsiCo, Inc., 238 F. Supp. 2d at 1176; accord Operating Eng'rs Health & Welfare Tr. 
Fund for N. California v. Precision Drilling, Inc., 782 F. Supp. 3d 784, 790 (N.D. Cal. 2025). There 
is nothing in the record to suggest possible disputes concerning material facts – EDC has 
presented dated and itemized invoices for each delivery of the Goods. Nor is there any 
indication that Unix’s failure to respond to this action is due to excusable neglect. Unix was 
served with the Complaint and the order entering default. See Shanghai Automation 
Instrument Co., Ltd. v. Kuei, 194 F. Supp. 2d 995, 1005 (N.D. Cal. 2001) (finding no excusable 
neglect where the defendants were served and failed to respond). 
Although the policy favoring resolution of the case on the merits always weighs against default 
judgment, this preference standing alone is not dispositive. PepsiCo, Inc., 238 F. Supp. 2d at 
1177. Unix’s failure to answer makes a decision on the merits «impractical, if not impossible,» 
and does not preclude the Court from entering default judgment against it. Id. 
* * * 
Upon balancing the Eitel factors, the Court concludes they weigh in favor of granting EDC’s 
motion for default judgment. 
C. Remedies 
1. Damages 
In the event of the buyer’s breach of contract, the CISG provides the seller may inter alia 
«require the buyer to pay the price.» CISG, arts. 61, 62. «Damages for breach of contract by 
one party consist of a sum equal to the loss, including loss of profit, suffered by the other party 
as a consequence of the breach. Such damages may not exceed the loss which the party in 
breach foresaw or ought to have foreseen at the time of the conclusion of the contract, in the 
 
4 The CISG does not define liquidated damages. Under California law, liquidated damages are «an amount of 
compensation to be paid in the event of a breach of contract, the sum of which is fixed and certain by 
agreement.» Chodos v. W. Publ’g Co., 292 F.3d 992, 1002 (9th Cir. 2002) (quoting Kelly v. McDonald, 98 Cal. App. 
121, 125, 276 P. 404 (1929)). 
5 Upon review of the invoices, the Court notes the Exhibit attached to EDC’s complaint also includes 29 invoices 
from «Trans-Pro-Logistic Inc.» to Unix for net freight charges. Based upon the Court’s calculations, EDC did not 
include these freight charges in their calculation of damages. Accordingly, the Court will disregard the «Trans-
Pro-Logistic Inc.» invoices. 
14  
15  
16  
17  
18  


CISG-online 7751 
 
5 
 
light of the facts and matters of which he then knew or ought to have known, as a possible 
consequence of the breach of contract.» CISG, art. 74. 
Here, EDC seeks compensatory damages in the principal sum of $835,418.60 based upon the 
invoices. Upon review of the invoices, the Court finds this sum is the accurate total of the 
invoices, and each invoice is for the Goods at issue in the relevant time period between the 
relevant entities. See generally Compl., Exhibit 1; accord Alameda Elec. Distributors, Inc. v. Eco 
Eng'g Inc., No. CV 24-06754-HSG, --- F. Supp. 3d ---, 2025 WL 3079873, at *9 (N.D. Cal. Nov. 4, 
2025) (entering default judgment awarding compensatory damages in the liquidated sum of 
invoices). 
2. Pre-Judgment Interest 
The CISG provides for pre-judgment interest. «If a party fails to pay the price or any other sum 
that is in arrears, the other party is entitled to interest on it, without prejudice to any claim 
for damages recoverable under article 74.» CISG, art. 78. The CISG is silent as to what the 
applicable interest rate should be. EDC calculates the statutory pre-judgment interest due as 
$127,037.68. EDC’s calculation applies an interest rate of 10% per annum, taken from 
California Civil Code 3289, applied pro rata based upon the number of days between the 
invoice’s due date and a hearing date of October 31, 2025 or December 12, 2025.6 
The CISG is federal law, however, so the federal pre-judgment interest rate is better suited. 
Chateau des Charmes Wines Ltd., 328 F.3d at 530; accord Vanport Int'l, Inc. v. DFC Wood Prods. 
Pty Ltd., No. CV 22-01041-HZ, 2024 WL 261753, at *4 (D. Or. Jan. 24, 2024) (ordering 
recalculation for a CISG claim utilizing the federal interest rate). There is no binding authority 
on which pre-judgment interest rate applies to CISG claims. See Supermicro Computer, Inc. v. 
Digitechnic, S.A., 145 F. Supp. 2d 1147, 1151 (N.D. Cal. 2001) («case law interpreting and 
applying the CISG is sparse»). Pre-judgment interest «is a substantive aspect of a plaintiff’s 
claim, rather than a merely procedural mechanism.» In re Exxon Valdez, 484 F.3d 1098, 1101 
(9th Cir. 2007). «[F]ederal law may apply to the calculation of prejudgment interest when a 
substantive claim derives from federal law alone.» Oak Harbor Freight Lines, Inc. v. Sears 
Roebuck, & Co., 513 F.3d 949, 961 (9th Cir. 2008). Because the CISG is federal law, and the 
Court has federal question jurisdiction, the Court shall apply the federal interest rate.7 The 
 
6 EDC’s calculation includes pre-judgment interest of $282.30 on the invoice dated February 7, 2024. This invoice 
was a $1,600.00 credit issued from Kefiplant to Unix for damaged goods. EDC shall not include interest on this 
credit in its recalculation of pre-judgment interest. 
7 The Ninth Circuit made clear in Chateau des Charmes Wines Ltd. that the CISG is federal law. Chateau des 
Charmes Wines Ltd., 328 F.3d at 530 («there is no doubt that [the CISG] is valid and binding federal law.»). The 
Court therefore has federal question jurisdiction under 28 U.S.C. section 1331(a). Accord Asante Techs., Inc. v. 
PMC-Sierra, Inc., 164 F. Supp. 2d 1142, 1147 (N.D. Cal. 2001) (finding the CISG confers federal question 
jurisdiction). At the hearing, EDC cited to Chicago Prime Packers, Inc., where the district court in the Seventh 
Circuit applied the state interest rate. Chicago Prime Packers, Inc. v. Northam Food Trading Co., 320 F. Supp. 2d 
702, 716 (N.D. Ill. 2004), aff'd, 408 F.3d 894 (7th Cir. 2005). The district court did so, however, because that case 
invoked diversity jurisdiction. Id. EDC also cited to Jae Yeon Textile Inc., where the district court awarded pre-
judgment interest at California’s interest rate. Jae Yeon Textile Inc. v. AKM Textile Inc., No. CV 16-05349-SJO 
(JEMx), 2017 WL 7156244, at *6 (C.D. Cal. Nov. 27, 2017). The district court applied this rate because it found 
19  
20  
21  


CISG-online 7751 
 
6 
 
Ninth Circuit uses the interest rate in 28 U.S.C. section 1961 to fix the rate of pre-judgment 
interest. Van Asdale v. Int'l Game Tech., 763 F.3d 1089, 1093 (9th Cir. 2014). 
EDC shall perform new calculations for pre-judgment interest using the federal rate under 
28 U.S.C. section 1961 and submit them for the Court’s review. See 28 U.S.C. § 1961(a) (setting 
forth how to determine the applicable rate), (b) (setting forth how to calculate interest, 
including annual compounding). 
3. Costs 
EDC requests costs under 28 U.S.C. section 1920. Specifically, EDC requests $415 for the 
Complaint’s filing fee and $105 for a process server’s fee.  Under Rule 54(d), «unless a federal 
statute, these rules, or a court order provides otherwise, costs ... should be allowed to the 
prevailing party.» Fed. R. Civ. P. 54(d). Fees paid to the Clerk in connection with a case, and 
reasonable fees for service, are taxable costs. C.D. Cal. L.R. 54-3. The Court therefore awards 
costs in the sum of $520. 
III. Conclusion 
In light of the foregoing, EDC’s MDJ is GRANTED in part. EDC shall file a supplement to its 
motion for default judgment detailing the recalculation of the pre-judgment interest, and a 
proposed judgment, by December 19, 2025. The new calculation shall assume a date of 
judgment of December 19, 2025, and include pre-judgment interest and costs consistent with 
this Order. 
IT IS SO ORDERED. 
 
 
«no compelling reason to deviate» from plaintiff’s request, and did not substantively address the question of 
whether the federal rate should apply instead. Id.

23
